title: Hortobágyi palacsinta
subtitle: Hungarian savoury pancakes with chicken paprikash filling
excerpt: Thin crêpes filled with a paprika chicken ragù, rolled and baked in a soured cream sauce. One of the few savoury pancake dishes in European cuisine serious enough to serve at a dinner table.
sourceNotes: Hortobágyi palacsinta takes its name from the Hortobágy puszta, the great Hungarian plain, and the dish was traditionally associated with herdsmen and cattle drovers who filled the pancakes with the meat from their stews. The recipe became widely known outside Hungary in the twentieth century when it was adopted by Hungarian restaurants as a starter or main course. The pancakes are filled, rolled, and baked in the sauce — the finished dish is quite different from the Breton crêpe tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the filled, rolled pancakes before adding the sauce. Defrost overnight, pour the sauce over, and bake from cold, covered, at 170°C for 25 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The filling and sauce double well. Make a larger batch of filled pancakes for the freezer.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made up to 2 days ahead. Cook the pancakes and fill on the day. The assembled dish can be refrigerated for up to 8 hours before baking.

content.text: These are not dessert pancakes with a savoury note. The palacsinta here is a vehicle for a proper paprika chicken filling — the crêpe is thin and eggy but its sole purpose is to hold the ragù together while the whole thing bakes in soured cream. The result is something between a pasta bake and a stuffed crêpe: substantial, rich, and unmistakably Hungarian in flavour.

The filling must be quite dry before it goes into the pancakes — a wet filling soaks through the crêpe during baking. Cook the chicken filling until the sauce is thick enough to hold its shape when spooned.
Make the pancake batter and cook

Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{eggs}}, {{whole-milk}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} until smooth. Rest for 30 minutes. Cook 8 thin crêpes in a lightly buttered pan, stacking them on a plate as they are done.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a frying pan over medium heat. Cook the {{onion}} for 8 minutes until soft. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{paprika-sweet}}. Return to the heat and add the {{chicken-thigh}}. Cook for 10 minutes, stirring, until the chicken is cooked through and the mixture is relatively dry. Stir in the {{soured-cream}}, season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}, and cook for 2 minutes more until thick. Leave to cool slightly.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Place a heaped tablespoon of filling near the edge of each crêpe, roll tightly, and place seam-side down in a lightly buttered baking dish. Whisk the sauce {{soured-cream}}, {{plain-flour}}, and {{paprika-sweet}} together until smooth. Pour over the rolls. Bake uncovered for 20 minutes until the sauce is set and the tops are lightly coloured. Serve immediately.

items.cause: Too thin, or not rested long enough
items.fix: The batter benefits from a 30-minute rest. Cook the crêpes on medium-low heat and do not rush the first side — lift an edge to check before turning

items.cause: Soured cream overheated without the flour to stabilise it
items.fix: Ensure the tablespoon of flour is fully whisked into the cream before pouring. The starch stabilises the cream during baking

items.symptom: Filling too wet and soaking through the crêpe
items.fix: Continue cooking the filling until the cream sauce has reduced to a thick coating on the chicken. It should hold its shape when dropped from a spoon

content.text: Hortobágyi palacsinta occupies a specific place in Hungarian cuisine: it is a restaurant dish that also works at home, serious enough for a dinner party but familiar enough for a Sunday lunch. It was served at the 1958 World's Fair in Brussels as one of Hungary's defining dishes, and it remains on the menus of Hungarian restaurants in every country where Hungarian emigrants settled. The paprika filling is the key: without that depth of spice, it would just be a crêpe.
